tossups.question: A satire published under a partial anagram of this composer’s name teaches people like poets, composers, singers, and female singers’ mothers how to create modern opera. This composer’s name was fraudulently attached to a collection of musette sonatas by Nicolas Chédeville titled The Faithful Shepherd. This composer’s opus 3 concerti were transcribed for keyboard by a court composer in Weimar. In 1939, interest in this composer’s music was (*) revived by Alfredo Casella’s “Week” in his honor. In 2012, Daniel Hope premiered a minimalist “recomposition” of one of this composer’s works by Max Richter. This composer taught violin and mandolin at the Ospedale della Pietà, an orphanage for whose choir he wrote his D major Gloria. For 10 points, name this Venetian “Red Priest” who wrote The Four Seasons.
tossups.question_sanitized: A satire published under a partial anagram of this composer's name teaches people like poets, composers, singers, and female singers' mothers how to create modern opera. This composer's name was fraudulently attached to a collection of musette sonatas by Nicolas Chedeville titled The Faithful Shepherd. This composer's opus 3 concerti were transcribed for keyboard by a court composer in Weimar. In 1939, interest in this composer's music was (*) revived by Alfredo Casella's "Week" in his honor. In 2012, Daniel Hope premiered a minimalist "recomposition" of one of this composer's works by Max Richter. This composer taught violin and mandolin at the Ospedale della Pieta, an orphanage for whose choir he wrote his D major Gloria. For 10 points, name this Venetian "Red Priest" who wrote The Four Seasons.
tossups.answer: Antonio Vivaldi [accept Vivaldi week] (The first sentence refers to Benedetto Marcello’s Il teatro alla moda, which merged Vivaldi and singer Caterina Canteli into the persona of “Aldiviva Licante.” The court composer in the third sentence is J. S. Bach.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Antonio Vivaldi [accept Vivaldi week] (The first sentence refers to Benedetto Marcello's Il teatro alla moda, which merged Vivaldi and singer Caterina Canteli into the persona of "Aldiviva Licante." The court composer in the third sentence is J. S. Bach.)

tossups.question: Kangmin Justin Kim’s drag alter ego sang an aria by this composer, parodying Cecilia (“chay-CHEEL-ya”) Bartoli’s crazed coloratura in “Agitata da due venti.” This composer wrote an opera about emperor Anastasio’s jealousy of the title peasant that contains the signature aria of the breakdancing countertenor Jakub Józef Orliński, “Vedrò con mio diletto.” This composer of Griselda included Geminiano Giacomelli’s aria “Sposa son disprezzata” in his pasticcio Bajazet. The (*) sinfonias from this composer’s operas Il Giustino and Dorilla in Tempe feature transposed versions of the melody “pickup E, G-sharp, G-sharp, G-sharp, F-sharp, E, high B,” which he recycled for a concerto in which the violinist’s trilled Bs represent birdsong. For 10 points, what director of the Ospedale della Pietà wrote The Four Seasons?
tossups.question_sanitized: Kangmin Justin Kim's drag alter ego sang an aria by this composer, parodying Cecilia ("chay-CHEEL-ya") Bartoli's crazed coloratura in "Agitata da due venti." This composer wrote an opera about emperor Anastasio's jealousy of the title peasant that contains the signature aria of the breakdancing countertenor Jakub Jozef Orlinski, "Vedro con mio diletto." This composer of Griselda included Geminiano Giacomelli's aria "Sposa son disprezzata" in his pasticcio Bajazet. The (*) sinfonias from this composer's operas Il Giustino and Dorilla in Tempe feature transposed versions of the melody "pickup E, G-sharp, G-sharp, G-sharp, F-sharp, E, high B," which he recycled for a concerto in which the violinist's trilled Bs represent birdsong. For 10 points, what director of the Ospedale della Pieta wrote The Four Seasons?
tossups.answer: Antonio Vivaldi (Kim sang “Agitata da due venti” as Kimchilia Bartoli.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Antonio Vivaldi (Kim sang "Agitata da due venti" as Kimchilia Bartoli.)

tossups.question: A composer with this first name accompanied Philip II on his travels throughout Europe, where he influenced English virginal composers with his variations on themes like canto del Caballero. A composer with this first name was called "the Devil dressed as a priest" for his controversial treatise The Key to Modulation. Most of the works of a composer with this first name were posthumously published by his son Hernando; that blind organist had the surname de Cabezón. A composer with this first name wrote six concertos for two organs and around (*) 150 keyboard sonatas. Most of Bach's Weimar concertos transcribe pieces by a composer with this first name for keyboard. Composers with this first name include one surnamed Soler and another who wrote a C major mandolin concerto for a prodigy he taught at an all-girls school. For 10 points, give this first name of the director of the Ospedale della Pietà.
tossups.question_sanitized: A composer with this first name accompanied Philip II on his travels throughout Europe, where he influenced English virginal composers with his variations on themes like canto del Caballero. A composer with this first name was called "the Devil dressed as a priest" for his controversial treatise The Key to Modulation. Most of the works of a composer with this first name were posthumously published by his son Hernando; that blind organist had the surname de Cabezon. A composer with this first name wrote six concertos for two organs and around (*) 150 keyboard sonatas. Most of Bach's Weimar concertos transcribe pieces by a composer with this first name for keyboard. Composers with this first name include one surnamed Soler and another who wrote a C major mandolin concerto for a prodigy he taught at an all-girls school. For 10 points, give this first name of the director of the Ospedale della Pieta.
tossups.answer: Antonio [accept Antonio de Cabezón; accept Antonio Soler; accept Antonio Vivaldi]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Antonio [accept Antonio de Cabezon; accept Antonio Soler; accept Antonio Vivaldi]

tossups.question: A G minor flute concerto by this composer with six alternating largo and presto movements includes movements titled for “ghosts” and “sleep.” Women sing all the roles of this composer’s only surviving oratorio, which allegorizes a military victory at Corfu. Violent sixteenth notes on open G strings open the Presto finale of a concerto by this composer. A set of six flute concerti by this composer includes pieces titled The Goldfinch and The Storm at Sea. This composer’s Mandolin Concerto in C major is often paired with a collection that includes a piece that uses a two-note short-long viola motif to represent a barking dog. This composer published a collection of violin concerti in The Contest Between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this Italian composer of The Four Seasons.
tossups.question_sanitized: A G minor flute concerto by this composer with six alternating largo and presto movements includes movements titled for "ghosts" and "sleep." Women sing all the roles of this composer's only surviving oratorio, which allegorizes a military victory at Corfu. Violent sixteenth notes on open G strings open the Presto finale of a concerto by this composer. A set of six flute concerti by this composer includes pieces titled The Goldfinch and The Storm at Sea. This composer's Mandolin Concerto in C major is often paired with a collection that includes a piece that uses a two-note short-long viola motif to represent a barking dog. This composer published a collection of violin concerti in The Contest Between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this Italian composer of The Four Seasons.
tossups.answer: Antonio Vivaldi (The oratorio in the second clue is Juditha Triumphans.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Antonio Vivaldi (The oratorio in the second clue is Juditha Triumphans.)

tossups.question: A reconstructed setting of Ecce nunc by a protégée of this location was premiered by the Australian Chamber Choir in 2022. A protégée of this location was immediately hired in Bayreuth as a “chamber music virtuosa,” where she wrote many flute sonatas and divertimenti. The opera Domina in Tempe was the first collaboration between a protégée of this location and a composer who was there due to a dispensation from mass for asthma. The composer of Tigrane also taught Domenico Scarlatti while supervising this institution. A visiting poet wrote that “angels [dared] hover near” a (*) mandolin prodigy at this institution who was featured in pieces such as one composer’s RV 425. That composer wrote two concerti per month for this institution’s figlie di coro (“FEEL-yeh di COH-roh”), who stayed behind a metal grate when they premiered pieces such as L’estro armonico. For 10 points, name this Venetian orphanage whose students learned music from Antonio Vivaldi.
tossups.answer: Ospedale della Pietà [or Hospital of Mercy; prompt on Antonio Vivaldi’s orphanage or convent by asking “what was its name?”] (The composers in the first four clues are Agata della Pietà, Anna Bon, Anna Girò, and Francesco Gasparini.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Ospedale della Pieta [or Hospital of Mercy; prompt on Antonio Vivaldi's orphanage or convent by asking "what was its name?"] (The composers in the first four clues are Agata della Pieta, Anna Bon, Anna Giro, and Francesco Gasparini.)

tossups.question: An oratorio by this composer allegorizes a military victory by Johann Matthias von der Schulenburg at Corfu. This composer’s solo vocal music, such as “Cessate, omai cessate,” (ches-SA-te, oh-ma-EE ches-SA-te) was defended in a book on his chamber cantatas by musicologist Michael Talbot. The sixth movement of this composer’s most popular choral piece is scored for soprano, continuo, and oboe or violin alone, and is a setting of the words (*) “Domine deus, rex coelestis” (“chay-LESS-tees”). An alto plays Holofernes in his only surviving oratorio, Juditha Triumphans, which is numbered 644 in the RV catalog of his works and was written while he worked at the Ospedale della Pietà orphanage for girls. For 10 points, name this Italian baroque composer of a Gloria in D Major, as well as The Four Seasons.

For 10 points, name this Italian baroque composer of a Gloria in D Major, as well as The Four Seasons.

tossups.question: Two answers required. The interest of one of these composers in the works of the other was as a ‘harmonic laboratory’, according to a work titled for one of these composers and the Patterns of Invention. The only four-harpsichord concerto by one of these composers was a transcription of one of the other’s works, one of 22 keyboard transcriptions made by them from the other’s violin concertos. Those works were written after Johann Ernst of Saxe-Weimar brought the latter’s first set of published concertos, L’estro armonico, from Venice. One of these composers wrote a concerto featuring flauti d’echo, while the other imitated a dog barking in a programmatic concerto. For 10 points, name these two composers, one of the Four Seasons, the other of the Brandenburg Concertos.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Antonio Vivaldi and Johann Sebastian Bach [prompt on partial answers]

tossups.question: Diego Fasolis and Fabio Ceresa have collaborated to produce several of this composer's operas; most recently, a pastoral opera in which this composer cast his longtime collaborator Anna Girò. Though this composer claimed to have written 94 operas, less than half of them survive, with many partly reconstructed thanks to Alberto Gentili's discovery of his manuscripts. Like Handel, this composer of Dorilla in Tempe wrote a dramma per musica adaptation of Ariosto's epic poem (*) Orlando Furioso. The librettist Anna Bon was one of this composer's orphaned pupils at the Ospedale della Pietà ("ohs-peh-DAH-lay del-lah pee-eh-TAH"). He is best-known today for a set of orchestral pieces which depict buzzing flies, barking dogs, and thunderstorms, each accompanied by a sonnet. For 10 points, name this "Red Priest" who composed The Four Seasons.

The librettist Anna Bon was one of this composer's orphaned pupils at the Ospedale della Pieta ("ohs-peh-DAH-lay del-lah pee-eh-TAH"). He is best-known today for a set of orchestral pieces which depict buzzing flies, barking dogs, and thunderstorms, each accompanied by a sonnet. For 10 points, name this "Red Priest" who composed The Four Seasons.

tossups.question: This composer wrote the virtuoso sacred motet “In furore iustissimae irae.” A 12-section choral work by this composer opens with trumpet, oboe, and strings leaping between two octave Ds in a low–high–low–low pattern and was lost until the 1920s. Cellos open a piece by this composer with a bar of slow staccato eighth notes on F, then higher strings join in each successive bar to build up the chord F, G, D-flat, and B-flat with quick mordents. In 1939, Alfredo Casella popularized this composer’s setting of the (*) Gloria in D major. This composer’s Mandolin Concerto in C major is often paired with four concerti grossi he published with sonnets. Rapid ascending minor scales and tremolos depict thunderstorms in the first two works in this composer’s collection The Contest Between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this Italian Baroque composer of The Four Seasons.

tossups.question: This composer’s supposed authorship of a serenata titled “Andromeda Liberata” caused a feud between Olivier Foures (oh-LIV-yay foo-RAY) and Michael Talbot, the latter of whom is considered the preeminent scholar of this composer. This composer’s only concerto featuring trumpets is his Concerto for Two Trumpets in C Major. The two surviving settings of this composer’s Gloria are entries 588 and 589 in the Ryom–Verzeichnis catalogue of his work. He established the norm for the ritornello form, and he marked the viola section of one of his violin concertos with the phrase “the barking dog.” This composer collected twelve concertos, including Pleasure and The Hunt, in a set of pieces titled The Contest between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this “Red Priest” who composed The Four Seasons.

This composer collected twelve concertos, including Pleasure and The Hunt, in a set of pieces titled The Contest between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this "Red Priest" who composed The Four Seasons.

tossups.question: Previously unknown works by this composer were featured at the Concerti Tigulliani organized by Olga Rudge and Ezra Pound, who helped popularize his music. Fritz Kreisler wrote a C major concerto that he falsely attributed to this composer. Alfredo Casella organized a week in 1939 honoring him, reviving his now-popular Gloria. His works were most thoroughly catalogued by Peter Ryom, who gave them their RV numbers. Bach’s Concerto for Four Harpsichords is an adaptation of the tenth piece in this composer’s L’Estro Armonico. He imitated the barking of a dog in the first of the programmatic violin concerti that open his The Contest Between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this Italian violinist of the Baroque era who composed The Four Seasons.

tossups.question: A concerto by this composer was reworked by J.S. Bach into Concerto for Four Harpsichords. After Torelli, this composer first innovated with the ritornello form by shortening inner ritornello sections and introducing modulation in them, rather than solely in the solo sections, as well as returning to the tonic in the last ritornello. His most popular sacred work is a setting of the (*) Gloria in excelsis deo, in D major. The last movement of a popular piece by him imitates the drones of bagpipes with sustained notes in the low strings. In another popular piece, he used violin pizzicatos to suggest rain "plopping". Those popular pieces by this composer of the Concerto Alla Rustica are found in Contest Between Harmony and Invention. For 10 points, name this Baroque, Italian composer of the Four Seasons.

parts: Name this composer. At the end of Portrait of a Lady on Fire, the camera cuts from Marianne and zooms-in on a teary-eyed Héloïse sitting in a theater box while this composer’s music plays in the background.

A Vivaldi violin concerto accompanies music by Handel and Purcell in the Baroque soundtrack to the period drama The Favourite, which was directed by a filmmaker from this country. A composer from this country, Vangelis, created the soundtrack to the film Chariots of Fire.

Vivaldi’s Concerto for Two Horns accompanies a shot of a distant funeral in a film by this director, in which a filmmaker and his son drive around the countryside while searching for two actors in the aftermath of an earthquake.
parts_sanitized: Name this composer. At the end of Portrait of a Lady on Fire, the camera cuts from Marianne and zooms-in on a teary-eyed Heloise sitting in a theater box while this composer's music plays in the background.

A Vivaldi violin concerto accompanies music by Handel and Purcell in the Baroque soundtrack to the period drama The Favourite, which was directed by a filmmaker from this country. A composer from this country, Vangelis, created the soundtrack to the film Chariots of Fire.

Vivaldi's Concerto for Two Horns accompanies a shot of a distant funeral in a film by this director, in which a filmmaker and his son drive around the countryside while searching for two actors in the aftermath of an earthquake.
answers: Antonio Vivaldi (The music played is “Summer” from The Four Seasons.)
Greece [or the Hellenic Republic; or Ellinikí Dimokratía] (The unnamed filmmaker is Yorgos Lanthimos.)
Abbas Kiarostami (The film is Life, and Nothing More…)
answers_sanitized: Antonio Vivaldi (The music played is "Summer" from The Four Seasons.)
Greece [or the Hellenic Republic; or Elliniki Dimokratia] (The unnamed filmmaker is Yorgos Lanthimos.)
Abbas Kiarostami (The film is Life, and Nothing More...)

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about 18th-century opera in the Americas, for 10 points each.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about 18th-century opera in the Americas, for 10 points each.
parts: This composer’s rival Domenico Zipoli wrote San Ignacio de Loyola as a Jesuit missionary in Paraguay. This composer’s own works include the 1733 opera Motezuma, a D-major Gloria, and the collection L’estro armonico.

This character’s death ends the opera La púrpura de la rosa, which premiered in Lima in 1701. Henry Purcell’s (“PUR-sull’s”) Dido and Aeneas is modeled on John Blow’s opera about this character and his lover.

Popular in the colonial United States was this satirical opera that features folk songs like “Over the Hills and Far Away.” Near this opera’s end, a condemned man laments his fate to the tune of “Greensleeves.”
parts_sanitized: This composer's rival Domenico Zipoli wrote San Ignacio de Loyola as a Jesuit missionary in Paraguay. This composer's own works include the 1733 opera Motezuma, a D-major Gloria, and the collection L'estro armonico.

This character's death ends the opera La purpura de la rosa, which premiered in Lima in 1701. Henry Purcell's ("PUR-sull's") Dido and Aeneas is modeled on John Blow's opera about this character and his lover.

Popular in the colonial United States was this satirical opera that features folk songs like "Over the Hills and Far Away." Near this opera's end, a condemned man laments his fate to the tune of "Greensleeves."
answers: Adonis [accept Venus and Adonis] (Tomás de Torrejón y Velasco composed La púrpura de la rosa, or The Blood of the Rose.)
answers_sanitized: Adonis [accept Venus and Adonis] (Tomas de Torrejon y Velasco composed La purpura de la rosa, or The Blood of the Rose.)

packet.name: A. Prelims 1 - Claremont A, Columbia A, Cornell A, MIT A

bonuses.leadin: This genre, which is usually set in 6/8 or 12/8 time, was originally popularized as a form of pastoral aria in Alessandro Scarlatti’s operas Telemaco and Griselda. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this genre. A piece in this genre transcribed for cello and piano by Gabriel Fauré was taken from his incidental music to Pelléas and Mélisande.

Composer and genre required. Fauré composed a cadenza for one of these pieces, the preceding one of which includes a second movement siciliana in the key of F-sharp minor. The twenty-sixth of these pieces is nicknamed “Coronation.”
Another siciliana appears in a Bach-transcribed concerto grosso by this composer, who included it in his collection L’estro armonico. This composer wrote The Four Seasons.

bonuses.leadin: Blind Celtic harpist Turlough O’Carolan (“TUR-lock oh-CAROL-in”) is said to have been challenged to perform the fifth piece from this collection on the spot; he did so perfectly, despite never having actually heard it before. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this set of twelve concertos, each of which is scored for four violins, two violas, cello, and continuo. Bach transcribed three of these concertos for solo harpsichord and two of them for organ.
About a decade after publishing L’estro armonico, this Italian composer wrote another set of twelve violin concertos, the first four of which are called The Four Seasons.

Vivaldi composed this setting of text from the Latin Mass for the Ospedale della Pietà. It reworks material from a similar piece by Giovanni Maria Ruggieri (“roo-JAIR-ee”) in its “Cum Sancto Spiritu” section.

bonuses.leadin: Though many forms exist, a common version of this musical motif begins with a [read slowly] "minor one, major five, minor one, major flat seven" progression. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Renaissance-era musical theme of possible Iberian origin. One of the most well-known themes throughout Europe prior to the 19th century, this theme has been used for variations by Marin Marais, Jean-Baptiste Lully, and Antonio Salieri.

The folia theme was used in this country for "Sinclairvisan", a patriotic tune about murdered diplomat Malcolm Sinclair. It's not Italy, but an early Venetian manuscript of Spanish music that lists many villancicos ["viyan-sikos"] is found at the Carolina Rediviva in this country, whose other old books include the Düben collection and the Codex Argenteus.

The final entry in this composer's Opus 1 is a trio sonata for two violins and basso continuo on the folia theme. Other famous violin works by this Italian Baroque composer include a set of 12 violin concertos in The Contest Between Harmony and Invention.
answers: Kingdom of Sweden (the manuscript is the Cancionero de Upsala)
answers_sanitized: la folia [or follies of Spain; or folies d'Espagne]
Kingdom of Sweden (the manuscript is the Cancionero de Upsala)

bonuses.leadin: This composer wrote a concerto in G major for the unusual combination of two mandolins. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Italian composer, who wrote brief poems as footnotes to the violin concertos which comprise his series The Four Seasons.
The outer movements of Vivaldi’s Double Concerto for Two Mandolins are structured around these repeating sections, which were commonly used as instrumental refrains in baroque operas and concertos.

The harmony in Vivaldi’s concertos is sustained by this type of accompaniment, which was ubiquitous in the baroque era and typically consisted of an instrumental bass line written in figured bass notation, so a keyboard or lute player could realize chords above it.

bonuses.leadin: The melody of a piece for this instrument by Francisco Tárrega, the Grand Vals, was used in a popular Nokia ringtone. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this string instrument of Spanish origin. Its music often accompanies flamenco dancing along with palmas, or hand clapping.

Unlike his Mandolin Concerto in C major, this Baroque composer’s Lute Concerto in D major is usually played today on the guitar. His short Double Trumpet Concerto in C major, RV 537, is one of the earliest solo brass pieces.

This master Spanish guitarist, who refused to play with his ring finger or fingernails, composed Variations on a Theme of Mozart. His book Méthode pour la Guitare (“may-TUD poor la ghee-TAR”) was one of the most popular pedagogical books of the 19th century.
answers: Fernando Sor [or Ferran Sor; or Fernando Sors or Fernando Sort or Fernando Sorts]
answers_sanitized: guitar [or guitarra; accept classical guitar or acoustic guitar]
Fernando Sor [or Ferran Sor; or Fernando Sors or Fernando Sort or Fernando Sorts]

parts: Name this work, a set of 12 solos and duets for soprano, alto, and string accompaniment, that takes its text from a medieval religious poem.

bonuses.leadin: Two of these instruments unusually pitched in G are designated in the score as “flauti d’echo” in fourth Brandenburg Concerto. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this duct flute whose popularity peaked during the Baroque era. Today, this instrument is mainly used for children’s music education purposes, because its head works like a whistle, making it easy to play.

bonuses.leadin: In this work, a flute imitates birds, while horns represent the villainous title animal. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this “symphonic fairy tale” for orchestra and narrator by Sergei Prokofiev. The bouncy string section depicts the other title character of this work.

bonuses.leadin: This composer based his orchestral suite Rossiniana and his ballet La boutique fantasque on Rossini’s piano music. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 20th-century Italian composer who orchestrated his 3 Preludes on Gregorian Melodies for Church Windows. He also composed Trittico botticelliano and a trilogy of symphonic poems.

Mario Castelnuovo-Tedesco wrote 24 preludes and fugues for two of these Well-Tempered instruments after studying Sor’s Mozart Variations. Ginastera imitated this instrument at the end of his first Argentine Dance and used folk music in his sonata for it.

Alfredo Casella organized a weeklong 1939 festival reviving this composer’s music. The anti-modernist critic Alceo (“al-CHAY-oh”) Toni arranged this Baroque violinist’s Four Seasons for piano four-hands.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, name these Italian composers.
parts: This Baroque composer, nicknamed “The Red Priest,” wrote sonnets that accompany the set of violin concertos that comprise his most famous work, The Four Seasons.
This Baroque composer wrote 555 solo keyboard sonatas, including a notable collection of thirty pieces called the Essercizi. His father, Alessandro, was also a famous composer.

This twentieth-century piano composer pioneered electric music in works such as The Turandot Suite. He drew inspiration from Bach for many pieces including his Fantasia Contrappuntistica, which is an extended variation on the final fugue from The Art of the Fugue.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, name these Italian composers.

This Baroque composer wrote 555 solo keyboard sonatas, including a notable collection of thirty pieces called the Essercizi. His father, Alessandro, was also a famous composer.

This twentieth-century piano composer pioneered electric music in works such as The Turandot Suite. He drew inspiration from Bach for many pieces including his Fantasia Contrappuntistica, which is an extended variation on the final fugue from The Art of the Fugue.
